column5: Another month begun.
My life has settled down into such a routine — sometimes I even forget that I haven’t lived here forever.
Maybe not quite, but I am finding it more and more difficult to remember what home was like.
What a sad word.
I don’t suppose I shall ever have a home again.
As if this month hasn’t been bad enough, it would have to have one extra day in it!
I am too cold and wet to write much.
It has been raining constantly, with the result that we have had no fire and have had to make do with cold johnnycake and salt pork to eat.
The mud all around our camp is so deep that we cannot move around very much.
Just going over to Hannah’s tent today I got the remains of my boots so muddy that Grannie made me take them off before I came into the tent.
Even that did not help as my boots are so full of holes that my feet were just as filthy.
Grannie fights against dirt and mud constantly but I do not think she will win.
The boatmen sing constantly as we go along.
Even though the boats have sails, they mostly have to row because the wind is against us from the west and we are going into a very strong current.
This is actually a very pleasant way to travel.
Except for the insects.

When we put in to shore for our noon meal we are immediately attacked by the black flies and mosquitoes, and the flies descend in such droves to cover our food that it is hard to take a bite without crunching down on one of them.
Father is worrying.
The summer is wearing on and they have not yet finished surveying the lots.
It will surely be too late to plant crops by the time we are settled.
We will have to depend on Government supplies for the winter, Father says.
He hates that!
We have harvested some small bedraggled turnip roots and a fair crop of beans.
Father wants to trade with the Indians for more corn.
